stories.title: Show HN: FixBugs – Reproduce production bugs and verify fixes
stories.paragraph: FixBugs is an AI agent that ingests contextual data surrounding production bugs, reproduces them in a sandbox environment, and generates verified fixes. The tool is notable because it closes the loop between bug detection and confirmed resolution rather than merely suggesting patches. It is available both as a self-hosted VSCode extension and as a GitHub app, giving teams flexibility in how they integrate it into existing workflows.

stories.title: Show HN: Sx 2.0 – Share AI skills with your team through a Dropbox folder
stories.paragraph: Sx 2.0 is a tool that allows teams to share AI skills across clients by storing them in a Dropbox folder, removing the requirement for git-based storage that characterized its first version. The update was driven by user feedback revealing that non-technical users were increasingly adopting AI skills but lacked a practical sharing mechanism. The new approach supports sharing at personal, team, and organizational levels without requiring command-line familiarity.

stories.title: Cdbx.ai – AI-powered browser IDE to describe, build, and publish apps
stories.paragraph: Cdbx.ai is a browser-based IDE that lets users describe an application in natural language, generate it with AI assistance, and publish it directly from the same interface. The tool targets users who want to move from idea to deployed app without switching between separate editing, building, and hosting environments. Running entirely in the browser lowers the setup barrier compared with locally installed development tools.

stories.title: DOOMQL
stories.paragraph: Developer Peter Gostev built DOOMQL, a Doom-like game in which SQLite serves as the game engine rather than just a data store, handling movement, collision detection, enemy logic, combat, and pixel rendering entirely through SQL queries. The project was built using GPT-5.6 Sol and is implemented as a Python terminal application. It is a concrete demonstration of how far SQL can be pushed as a computational substrate when combined with current code-generation models.

stories.title: datasette code-frequency chart on GitHub
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison examined his own GitHub commit history for the Datasette open-source project to measure the practical impact of recent AI coding agents on his output. The code-frequency chart shows a pronounced spike in activity that Willison correlates with the availability of Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, Fable 5, and GPT-5.6 Sol. The analysis is a rare firsthand, data-backed account of how a specific developer's productivity changed with successive model generations.

stories.title: Show HN: I built a one-prompt hackathon platform, free entry, sponsored prizes
stories.paragraph: 1ShotChallenge.ai is a hackathon platform where participants submit a single prompt rather than a full project, with free entry and sponsor-funded prizes. The format compresses the traditional hackathon structure down to prompt engineering as the primary skill being evaluated. The platform's low barrier to entry distinguishes it from conventional hackathons that require working code submissions.

stories.title: MIT's New Method Flags AI Models Trained on CASM Without Generating It
stories.paragraph: Researchers at MIT have developed a technique to detect whether an AI model was trained on child sexual abuse material (CSAM) without requiring the model to generate any such content during the evaluation process. This is significant because prior detection approaches risked producing harmful outputs as a byproduct of probing model behavior. The method addresses a critical gap in AI model auditing, particularly as regulators and platforms increase scrutiny of training data provenance.

stories.title: Four awful new privacy-eroding features from Meta in a month
stories.paragraph: Within a single month, Meta introduced four features on Instagram that critics say erode user privacy, including the use of AI-powered facial recognition. The piece documents the changes systematically, noting that the rollout pace makes it difficult for users and regulators to respond to each feature in turn. Facial recognition in particular raises legal concerns in jurisdictions with biometric data protection laws such as Illinois's BIPA.

stories.title: Samsung Health app threatens data deletion if users opt out AI training
stories.paragraph: Samsung's Health app has begun warning users that opting out of AI training data sharing may result in deletion of their personal health data. The policy effectively makes data contribution to AI training a condition of continued access to one's own health records, a practice that consumer advocates have described as coercive. The situation highlights a growing tension between companies' AI data needs and users' rights to control sensitive personal information.

stories.title: Economists are coming around to the idea that AI really is killing jobs
stories.paragraph: A notable shift is occurring among economists who had previously been skeptical of AI-driven job displacement, with a growing number now acknowledging that current AI adoption is causing measurable employment losses. The article references a joint statement signed by economists drawing comparisons to the labor disruptions of the Industrial Revolution. Earlier consensus had held that automation typically creates as many jobs as it destroys, but recent labor market data is prompting researchers to revisit that assumption.

stories.title: The AI Whale Fall and Open Source
stories.paragraph: The piece uses the ecological metaphor of a whale fall — the process by which a dead whale's body sustains deep-sea ecosystems for years — to examine how large AI companies generate downstream economic and creative opportunities for the open-source community. The argument is that even as major labs consolidate resources, their outputs, discarded projects, and published research feed a broader ecosystem of developers and researchers. The framing offers a counterpoint to narratives that portray AI concentration as purely extractive relative to open-source contributors.

stories.title: AI is a bad tool
stories.paragraph: The essay argues that AI is categorically a poor tool for many software development tasks, contending that the probabilistic and opaque nature of language model outputs conflicts with the deterministic requirements of reliable software systems. The author positions the piece as a corrective to uncritical adoption narratives, drawing on specific failure modes rather than general skepticism. It joins a growing body of practitioner writing that documents where AI-assisted development adds friction rather than reducing it.

stories.title: Economists say 'we must act now' on AI's economic impact and job displacement
stories.paragraph: A group of economists is calling for immediate policy action to address AI-driven job displacement and its broader economic consequences. The appeal reflects growing concern among labor economists that automation enabled by AI systems is outpacing the social safety nets and retraining programs designed to support displaced workers. The economists argue that delayed intervention risks widening income inequality, and they are urging governments to consider measures such as expanded unemployment insurance, worker retraining initiatives, and revised tax structures. The statement adds institutional weight to an ongoing debate over how quickly AI adoption is reshaping labor markets across multiple sectors.
